title: Waffles
subtitle: Crisp, deep-pocketed Belgian-style waffles with butter and maple syrup
excerpt: Thick, yeasted waffles cooked in a hot waffle iron until the outside is properly crisp and the inside stays soft and slightly chewy. The pockets are designed to hold butter and maple syrup. The batter uses yeast rather than baking powder, which gives a more complex flavour and a better texture — requires an overnight rest but no more active work.
sourceNotes: The waffle iron and waffle tradition arrived in North America with Dutch and Belgian immigrants in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. By the nineteenth century waffles were a fixture of American domestic cooking. Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544) includes waffle recipes. The Belgian-style waffle with its deeper grid pattern was popularised in the United States following the 1964 World's Fair in New York.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cooked waffles freeze well. Cool completely, then freeze in a single layer before bagging. Reheat from frozen in a toaster or 200°C oven for 5 minutes until crisp again.
recipe.batchNotes: The batter keeps overnight in the fridge; cook as many as needed each morning.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter must rest overnight (or at least 8 hours) in the fridge for the yeast to develop. Mix the evening before.

content.text: Yeasted waffles are better than baking-powder waffles. The overnight fermentation develops a subtle complexity that no chemical leavener can replicate, and the structure is slightly more open and chewy in a way that holds up to butter and syrup without going soggy. The trade-off is that you must mix the batter the night before — but that takes ten minutes and requires no skill. In the morning, the batter is ready; the only work is heating the iron and cooking the waffles.

A hot waffle iron is non-negotiable. If the iron is not properly preheated, the batter will stick and the waffles will be pale and soft. Most waffle irons have an indicator light; wait for it to signal ready, then add the batter. The correct amount is just enough to fill the grid without overflowing when the lid closes. Do not open the iron for at least 3 minutes — the steam needs to escape before the waffle will release cleanly.
Mix the batter the night before

Whisk together the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{yeast-fast-action}} of fast-action yeast, {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar, and a good pinch of salt in a large bowl. In a jug, combine the warm {{whole-milk}} of milk, {{unsalted-butter}} of melted butter, and {{eggs}} eggs, whisking briefly to combine. Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and whisk until smooth. Cover the bowl with clingfilm or a plate and refrigerate overnight, or for at least 8 hours. The batter will bubble and rise slightly.

Remove the batter from the fridge and stir gently — it will have a loose, slightly bubbly consistency. Preheat the waffle iron according to its instructions and brush lightly with a little melted butter or neutral oil. When fully hot, ladle in enough batter to just fill the grid. Close the lid and cook for 4 to 5 minutes without opening until the steam has largely stopped and the waffle is golden and crisp. Open carefully and lift the waffle free with a fork. Keep warm in a low oven while you cook the remaining waffles. Serve with butter and maple syrup.

items.cause: Iron not hot enough, or not greased before cooking
items.fix: Preheat fully and brush with butter or oil before each waffle; do not try to open the iron before the steam has subsided or the waffle will tear

items.symptom: Waffles are pale and soft rather than crisp
items.cause: Iron too cool or waffle removed too early
items.fix: Wait until the steam stops before opening; a pale waffle that steams when cut has not been cooked long enough

items.symptom: Batter has not risen or bubbled overnight
items.cause: Yeast is old and inactive, or milk was too hot and killed it
items.fix: Use warm (not hot) milk — around 40°C; test your yeast by dissolving a pinch in warm water with sugar and watching for bubbles after 10 minutes

items.symptom: Waffles are too thin and not deep enough
items.cause: Not enough batter per waffle, or iron is a thin-grid style rather than a deep Belgian grid
items.fix: Fill the grid generously; a Belgian-style iron produces thick waffles, a standard American iron produces thinner ones

content.text: Waffles arrived in North America with Dutch and Belgian settlers and were a common domestic preparation by the eighteenth century, cooked in long-handled iron moulds held over an open fire. The electric waffle iron made them a practical everyday item from the early twentieth century onward, and they became fixed on the American breakfast table alongside pancakes — similar in ingredients but different in character, with the crisp grid and the deep pockets that hold syrup. The deeper Belgian-style waffle was introduced to a wider American audience at the 1964 World's Fair and gradually became the dominant home-cooking version.
